learning_objectives: Define psychological abnormality using the four Ds: deviance, distress, dysfunction, danger
Describe the structure and use of the DSM-5-TR
Compare categorical and dimensional approaches to classification

lecture_notes.introduction: What makes behavior abnormal? This question has no simple answer, but the field has developed frameworks for identifying, classifying, and understanding psychological disorders.
key_concepts: Four Ds of abnormality: deviance, distress, dysfunction, danger
Historical perspectives: supernatural, biological, psychological models
DSM-5-TR: multiaxial to single-axis system, reliability concerns
Dimensional alternatives: Research Domain Criteria (RDoC)
Comorbidity and differential diagnosis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case vignette exercise: apply the four Ds to three scenarios and discuss diagnostic ambiguity

review.value: The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders; provides standardized criteria for diagnosing psychological disorders
NIMH framework organizing disorders by underlying biological and cognitive dimensions rather than symptom clusters

learning_objectives: Differentiate among generalized anxiety disorder, panic disorder, phobias, social anxiety disorder, and OCD
Explain biological, cognitive, and behavioral models of anxiety
Describe evidence-based treatments for anxiety disorders

lecture_notes.introduction: Anxiety disorders are the most prevalent class of psychological disorders, affecting approximately 30% of adults at some point in their lives.
key_concepts: GAD: chronic, diffuse worry; associated with intolerance of uncertainty
Panic disorder: recurrent panic attacks with catastrophic misinterpretation of bodily sensations
Specific and social phobias: conditioning, observational learning, and prepared learning
OCD: obsessions (intrusive thoughts) and compulsions (ritualistic behaviors); separate from anxiety in DSM-5
Treatments: CBT with exposure therapy, SSRIs, combined approaches
lecture_notes.demonstration: Behavioral experiment demonstration: interoceptive exposure exercise for understanding panic symptoms

review.value: Systematic, gradual confrontation with feared stimuli; the gold standard behavioral treatment for anxiety disorders
Clark's model: panic attacks result from catastrophic misinterpretation of normal bodily sensations (e.g., heart pounding = heart attack)

class_schedule.topic: Mood Disorders: Depression and Bipolar Spectrum
learning_objectives: Describe the diagnostic criteria for major depressive disorder and bipolar I/II
Compare biological, cognitive, and interpersonal models of depression
Evaluate pharmacological and psychotherapeutic treatments for mood disorders

lecture_notes.introduction: Mood disorders are among the leading causes of disability worldwide. Understanding their etiology and treatment is essential for clinical practice.
key_concepts: MDD: five or more symptoms for two weeks including depressed mood or anhedonia
Bipolar I (full mania) vs. bipolar II (hypomania + depression)
Beck's cognitive triad: negative views of self, world, and future
Seligman's learned helplessness and hopelessness theory
Treatments: SSRIs, CBT, behavioral activation, lithium for bipolar, ECT for treatment-resistant cases
lecture_notes.demonstration: Thought record exercise: identify cognitive distortions in depressive thinking patterns

review.value: Depression involves negative automatic thoughts about oneself (worthless), the world (hostile), and the future (hopeless)
Evidence-based treatment that targets avoidance and inactivity by scheduling pleasurable and meaningful activities

learning_objectives: Describe positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia
Explain the dopamine hypothesis and its refinements
Discuss the diathesis-stress model and neurodevelopmental perspective

lecture_notes.introduction: Schizophrenia is among the most severe psychological disorders, affecting approximately 1% of the population worldwide. Modern understanding integrates biological vulnerability with environmental stressors.
key_concepts: Positive symptoms: hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech and behavior
Negative symptoms: flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia
Dopamine hypothesis: excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathway
Glutamate hypothesis and neurodevelopmental models
Treatments: antipsychotics (typical and atypical), psychosocial rehabilitation, family therapy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Auditory hallucination simulation: students wear headphones with voices while attempting to complete a task

review.value: Schizophrenia arises when genetic vulnerability (diathesis) interacts with environmental stressors (prenatal infection, childhood trauma, cannabis use)
Often more debilitating than positive symptoms; less responsive to medication; major barrier to functional recovery

learning_objectives: Describe the three DSM-5 clusters of personality disorders
Analyze the diagnostic challenges and controversies in personality disorder classification
Discuss treatment approaches including dialectical behavior therapy for BPD

lecture_notes.introduction: Personality disorders are enduring, inflexible patterns of inner experience and behavior that deviate from cultural expectations and cause impairment.
key_concepts: Cluster A (odd/eccentric): paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal
Cluster B (dramatic/erratic): antisocial, borderline, histrionic, narcissistic
Cluster C (anxious/fearful): avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive
Borderline personality disorder: emotional dysregulation, unstable relationships, identity disturbance
Dialectical behavior therapy: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: differentiate personality disorders from acute disorders and identify patterns across clusters

review.value: Linehan's dialectical behavior therapy combines CBT with mindfulness; the most empirically supported treatment for borderline personality disorder
Personality disorder traits are experienced as consistent with the self, making them harder to treat than ego-dystonic conditions

learning_objectives: Describe PTSD diagnostic criteria and risk/resilience factors
Differentiate among dissociative disorders including DID
Explain the relationship between psychological factors and somatic symptoms

lecture_notes.introduction: Trauma can have profound effects on psychological functioning. This session covers the spectrum from acute stress reactions to chronic dissociative and somatic conditions.
key_concepts: PTSD: intrusion, avoidance, negative cognitions/mood, hyperarousal
Complex PTSD and developmental trauma
Dissociative identity disorder: controversy and evidence
Somatic symptom disorder and illness anxiety disorder
Evidence-based trauma treatments: CPT, prolonged exposure, EMDR
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case formulation exercise: develop a biopsychosocial case conceptualization for a PTSD case

review.value: Evidence-based PTSD treatment involving repeated, systematic confrontation with trauma memories and avoided situations
Disruption in normally integrated functions of consciousness, memory, identity, or perception; thought to be a defense against overwhelming trauma

questions.question: A 28-year-old reports persistent worry about multiple life domains for over a year, difficulty sleeping, muscle tension, and irritability. She denies panic attacks but says she cannot stop worrying even when she tries. Which disorder best fits, and what two DSM-5-TR criteria distinguish it from normal worry?
questions.answer: Generalized anxiety disorder (GAD). The two key distinguishing criteria are: (1) excessive anxiety and worry occurring more days than not for at least 6 months about multiple events or activities, and (2) the person finds it difficult to control the worry. Normal worry is proportionate, controllable, and time-limited, whereas GAD worry is chronic, pervasive, and perceived as uncontrollable.

questions.question: Compare Beck's cognitive model of depression with the learned helplessness model. How does each explain the onset of depression, and what treatment implications follow from each?
questions.answer: Beck's model: Depression results from negative cognitive schemas activated by stressful events, producing the cognitive triad (negative views of self, world, future) and cognitive distortions (all-or-nothing thinking, overgeneralization). Treatment: cognitive restructuring to identify and challenge distorted thoughts. Learned helplessness (Seligman): Depression results from the perception that outcomes are uncontrollable, leading to motivational, cognitive, and emotional deficits. The reformulated model adds attributional style (internal, stable, global). Treatment: attribution retraining and behavioral activation to restore a sense of control.

rubric.key: Accurately describes Beck's model and treatment
Accurately describes learned helplessness and treatment

questions.question: Explain why negative symptoms of schizophrenia are often more prognostically significant than positive symptoms, and describe one reason they are harder to treat.
questions.answer: Negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia) are more prognostically significant because they predict poorer functional outcomes, including difficulty maintaining employment, relationships, and independent living. Positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions) respond relatively well to antipsychotic medication, but negative symptoms are harder to treat because current antipsychotics primarily block dopamine D2 receptors, which addresses positive symptoms. Negative symptoms may involve different neural mechanisms (e.g., prefrontal cortex hypofunction, glutamate dysfunction) that are not targeted by standard antipsychotics.

rubric.key: Explains why negative symptoms predict poorer outcomes
Contrasts with positive symptom treatment response

questions.question: Describe the biosocial theory underlying dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) for borderline personality disorder. What is the core dialectic that gives DBT its name?
questions.answer: Linehan's biosocial theory proposes that BPD arises from a biologically based emotional vulnerability (high sensitivity, intense reactions, slow return to baseline) combined with an invalidating environment (caregivers dismiss or punish emotional expressions). This transaction produces chronic emotional dysregulation. The core dialectic in DBT is between acceptance (validating the client's current experience and pain as understandable) and change (teaching new skills to manage emotions and behavior). The therapist simultaneously communicates 'you are doing the best you can' and 'you need to do better.'

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Case Conceptualization
major_assignments.description: Given a detailed clinical case vignette, develop a comprehensive biopsychosocial case conceptualization including differential diagnosis, etiological analysis, and evidence-based treatment plan. The case will involve comorbidity requiring you to prioritize and sequence interventions.
objectives: Apply DSM-5-TR diagnostic criteria to a complex clinical presentation
Develop a biopsychosocial formulation integrating multiple etiological factors
Create an evidence-based treatment plan with justification from research literature
Address comorbidity and treatment sequencing considerations
deliverables: Minimum 10 peer-reviewed references supporting the formulation and plan

criteria: Integrates biological, psychological, and social factors
Identifies predisposing, precipitating, perpetuating, and protective factors
Coherent theoretical model explaining symptom development
Logical sequencing addressing safety and severity first
Considers practical barriers and treatment engagement
Accurate and nuanced DSM-5-TR diagnostic formulation with appropriate differential
Evidence-based treatment plan with logical sequencing and measurable goals

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of diagnostic criteria and theoretical models

case_presentations.description: Group case analysis presentations applying diagnostic frameworks

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive clinical case conceptualization and treatment plan

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering anxiety, mood, and psychotic disorders

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam with emphasis on differential diagnosis and treatment planning

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. Case presentations require full group participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Case conceptualization papers will not be accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Case presentations are group assignments. Written case conceptualizations must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All written work must be original with proper citations. Fabrication of clinical material is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to DSM-5-TR (online or print), university library databases, and word processing software.
